Alright, you're back. Time to roll forward. Here's the deal. Over the years, I found that the best way to teach someone olfactory anchoring is to take them through the steps of NLP anchoring first. Let's get started right now. And there are five steps to NLP anchoring. Step number one is you want to choose a state. Now, this is where you actually set an intention for the emotional state that you want to anchor. Now, in our case, let's choose a state of absolute certainty as this is that one linchpin state that you absolutely must learn to trigger at will if you want to succeed in sales, business, basically in life in general. Now, theoretically you could choose any state. If you want to go back to this exercise and get at some point down the road, you can do that. But for right now, let's all choose a state of certainty. Alright, with that, we want to step number two now, which is to choose your focus. Now, essentially, this is where I want you to close your eyes, go back to a moment in your life when you are feeling absolutely certain. For example, right after you closed a really tough sale and you were basking in the afterglow of that feeling, we just felt like you can close anyone who's closed them. That's a perfect moment. You had that super confident feeling because you sounded awesome. You got over that hump and boom, the client bought you and wow, you feel just really great. That's the perfect example of what you want to focus on, a perfect moment to focus on. Alright, now once you've located that memory, I want you to actually create a vivid picture of it in your mind's eye. I want you to take in the whole scene. See all the players who were there, how they looked, how they were dressed, how the hair was styled, how the room itself actually looked. Right now, creating this type of picture internally where you're actually looking down at yourself from above, it's called being in a dissociated state and it's a crucial aspect of both goal setting and self-motivation. However, on the other side of the equation, we have this called being in an associated state. This is where you're actually inside your own body looking out for your own eyes at the scene. That's even more powerful for when you're setting an anchor. We'll get to that in a moment. Okay, for now though, I want you to want to step number three, which is to choose your physiology. Now, this is where I want you to actually change your physiology, meaning how you move and hold your body to match the exact physiology of the state that you're trying to anchor, which in this case is what? Absolute certainty. Right? So you want to make sure that you're standing certain, holding your head certain, even breathing certain, talking internally to yourself certain, essentially every aspect of your physiology down to your most minute gestures and facial expressions. If you're resonating with a state of absolute certainty. Now, one point I want to make here is that if you're sitting in an office right now and it's difficult to act this stuff out, I get it. So go through this lesson passively, meaning just sit, listen, watch and learn, but please make a mental note to yourself to go back to this lesson again when you're in private. And I actually want you to act this stuff out with NLP anchoring and for that matter, olfactory anchoring. It's one of those cases where more is more, not less is more. So you can really engage your body as well. You get it before right now in this evolution of your in-office, fine, just sit back and learn, but please make sure you go back to this and play through it again with your body being engaged to. All right? So with that, let's now want the step number four of NLP anchoring, which is to intensify the state that you chose. Now, let me explain this. What this involves is using your five sensory modalities, also as your five senses, right? To take the picture that you've created in your mind's eye from step number two, and you want to use your brain to manipulate that image in a way that intensifies the feeling of absolute certainty that creates inside of you. This is actually really easy to do just so you know. And it can be fun too if you're alone, not in front of other people, right? But first, let me take you through the five sensory modalities and explain how they work and how you use them. All right? Now, let's start with number one, your first modality, which is called your visual modality. Now, this is what you see both externally in the world, your vision, and also internally in your mind's eye. Now, in the latter case in your mind's eye, that picture could be from a memory that you have, right? It's a recall, or it could be from your imagination, meaning visual construction, or a combination of the two. The points that you can remember what you saw back in the day, or create something that's even more empowering to you. You get it? That's your visual modality. Now, number two, we have what's called your auditory modality, which is what you hear. And again, it's going to be both external, what you hear out in the world, and internal, what you want to make up that you're like you make up dialogue, right? And it has the same permutations as above, okay? Now, you can construct it, you can remember it, or a combination, right? That's number two. Now, number three, we have what's called your kinesthetic modality. Now, this is what you feel, again, both externally and internally, and of course, the same permutations as above. Number four, you have your gustatory modality, which is what you taste, again, both externally and internally, same permutations as above, right? And then number five, which is really important to us here, is your olfactory modality, which is what you smell both externally in the world, and a smell internally that you remember or construct or a combination of those two, right? Now, here's the way it works. For most of us, as human beings, we're not labradors, right? We tend to rely on the first three modalities to make sense of the world around us, with our visual modality as humans being typically the most dominant, followed by auditory and then kinesthetic. That being said, though, there's people out like my wife who is more of a kinesthetic, a feeler, but again, what happens with her as well is she still makes pictures, she hears things, but she tends to interpret the world in feelings. See, nothing with the human brain is ever exactly set in stone. For example, if you're a chef, then you're also going to be relying heavily on your what? Your gustatory modality, right? You have to be able to taste things. And if you're a perfumer, well, guess what? You have to rely on your olfactory modality, your sense of smell spot on, right? Now, this is a perfect point to stop. So I want you to make sure you have this stuff locked down. There's a lot of building blocks here. My only caveat is that if you're in a place where you couldn't play full out, please redo this lesson again when you're alone and you can really close your eyes, stand certain, act certain. We'll do this again in the later lesson as well, but I want you to have this building block. I want you to own it, all right? So take your quiz and quickly come back and I will see you in the next lesson.
